results.possibleIdentity: Kenneth Charles Knowlton (1931–2022) was a prominent American computer scientist, engineer, and pioneer in computer graphics and digital art. From 1962 to 1982, he was a member of the Computer Techniques Research Department at Bell Telephone Laboratories in Murray Hill, New Jersey. During the 1960s and 1970s, Knowlton developed several influential computer languages, including BEFLIX (1963)—one of the first programming languages for computer animation—and EXPLOR (1969) for generating patterns. He is widely recognized for his collaborations with artists to bridge the gap between technology and creativity. In 1966, he co-created the iconic 'Computer Nude' (Studies in Perception I) with Leon Harmon, which was the first computer-processed image to be widely publicized and served as a foundational piece for the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). His work was featured in major exhibitions such as 'The Machine as Seen at the End of the Mechanical Age' (1968) at MoMA. Throughout his career, he collaborated with avant-garde figures such as Stan VanDerBeek, Lillian Schwartz, and Nam June Paik, and he held approximately 20 U.S. patents related to computer graphics.
